weather: Today in Edmonds: Morning clouds break to mostly sunny skies by afternoon. High 61-63°F, low tonight 45-46°F. Rain chance near zero. Light NNW winds 5-10 mph. Clear evening ahead.

local_news.findings: The Edmonds City Council approved key contracts and budget amendments during its April 7 business meeting, including a $415,116 wastewater treatment plant gasification system support contract with Jacobs Engineering. The council also considered but ultimately declined to pursue a counterpetition in the Redelfs tree litigation matter, where a landmark tree was removed without a permit. Additionally, the council proclaimed April 9 as Local News Day to recognize the value of local journalism.

The South County Fire Board of Commissioners is working on redistricting proposals to incorporate Edmonds' 42,000+ residents into the Regional Fire Authority. Two plans are under consideration: Plan 1 would place almost all of Edmonds into a single district, while Plan 2 would split the city between two districts. The board will hold public hearings to gather community input before finalizing the redistricting plan.

Edmonds will begin a major sewer and storm pipe rehabilitation project on May 4, targeting 27 locations with failing infrastructure identified in a 2013 review. The $419,000 project will use cured-in-place pipe (CIPP) technology to repair aging sewer and storm pipes throughout the city.

The Washington State Court of Appeals reopened the Edmonds tree mandate case involving resident Nathan Rimmer, who sued the city over unconstitutional tree replacement requirements. The court had previously dismissed the case as moot after Rimmer sold his property, but his attorney filed a motion to reconsider the dismissal.

Snohomish County Sheriff Susanna Johnson announced her re-election campaign on April 27, highlighting her focus on public safety initiatives including employee investment, trust and transparency, and organizational resiliency. Recent police blotters show various minor incidents in Edmonds, including theft arrests and property damage reports.

openings.role: English Language Arts Teacher (0.6 FTE) - Long Term Sub
openings.summary: Temporary 0.6 FTE long-term substitute for grades 7-8 English Language Arts, May 4–June 15 2026. Requires WA teaching certificate with Elementary endorsement. Daily guest-teacher pay scales from $215–$260 depending on consecutive days worked.

Pool posting for multiple anticipated full-time vacancies for the 2026-2027 school year. 180-day contract, 12 sick + 3 personal days, SEBB benefits for ≥630 hrs. Requires valid WA teaching certificate with appropriate endorsement.

General pool for anticipated 2026-27 secondary vacancies (English, History, etc.). Applications reviewed as specific openings arise; complete application (letter, résumé, 2 references, transcripts) required. EEA contract, prorated FTE possible.

Ongoing applicant pool for future high-school English/History openings. Edmonds SD uses pool postings; principals review applications as vacancies occur. Strong equity focus and commitment to culturally responsive instruction expected.

District #417 posts certificated pools each January; principals hire directly from pool or post specific openings. BEST-supported induction program for new teachers. Covers Bothell, Kenmore, Woodinville.
District hires secondary teachers April–June and again in August. Apply to general pool for consideration; specific postings added later. Emphasis on inclusive, equitable learning environments.

Shoreline SD maintains rolling pools for secondary certificated staff. FastTrack application system; electronic confidential references required. District serves ~9,000 students in Shoreline & Lake Forest Park.

Certificated hiring pools posted spring/summer for 2026-27. District emphasizes equity and anti-racism; up to $1,000 training reimbursement for some positions. Contact personnel@mukilteo.wednet.edu for details.

career.notes: All districts within 30 miles of Edmonds are actively recruiting for 2026-27 secondary English and History positions, primarily through rolling pool postings rather than single-job ads. Snohomish and Everett have the most concrete salary data; other districts will release updated salary schedules later this spring. Ensure WA teaching certificate with appropriate endorsements is current, and prepare electronic references before applying.

global_news.findings: The United Arab Emirates has announced it will quit OPEC and OPEC+ after nearly 60 years of membership, dealing a significant blow to the oil cartel's influence. The UAE said the decision reflects its "long-term strategic and economic vision" and will provide more flexibility as it seeks to boost production capacity. Analysts describe this as "the beginning of the end of OPEC" as the UAE produces 2.9 million barrels daily and represents about 15% of the cartel's capacity. The move comes amid the ongoing Iran war and global energy crisis.

King Charles III delivered a historic address to the US Congress, receiving multiple standing ovations as he emphasized the "indispensable" UK-US partnership. Speaking in the Capitol, the King warned of "great uncertainty" and "more volatile, more dangerous" times, calling for continued NATO support and backing for Ukraine. The speech came during his four-day US state visit and appeared aimed at repairing recent tensions between the two nations, with Trump praising the UK as having the "richest inheritance one nation could give to another."

President Trump stated that Britain's King Charles agrees that Iran should never be allowed to have nuclear weapons, during a White House state dinner for the royal couple. Trump said "we're never going to let that opponent have a nuclear weapon" and claimed Charles "agrees with me even more than I do." The comments came as the US-Iran war continues and tensions remain high over Tehran's nuclear program, with oil prices surging above $113 per barrel due to the Strait of Hormuz closure.

The UAE's departure from OPEC is shaking up the global oil alliance that has influenced prices worldwide for decades. The move comes as OPEC has sought to manage oil production and prices, but the UAE has chafed at production limits imposed by the cartel. While the immediate impact is limited due to the ongoing Strait of Hormuz blockade, analysts warn this could lead to long-term changes in oil market dynamics and potentially lower prices but higher volatility.

OPEC and its allies will lose significant power over the oil market when the UAE leaves next month, though the remaining producer alliance is expected to stay together. The UAE's exit removes one of the cartel's most compliant members and reduces OPEC's collective capacity by about 15%. Sources indicate that while this weakens the group's market influence, Saudi Arabia and other members will likely maintain the alliance despite the setback.

intellectual_journals.findings: A sweeping investigation by ProPublica and FRONTLINE reveals that more than 300 U.S. citizens and lawful residents were arrested during immigration-enforcement sweeps and charged with crimes such as assaulting or interfering with federal agents. Yet, when video evidence was examined, the officers’ sworn statements repeatedly collapsed: over one-third of the cases were dismissed, never filed, or lost at trial. The pattern suggests a deliberate use of arrest as a political weapon—creating a chilling effect on protest even when convictions never materialise.

In a lyrical 7,000-word essay, Maria Popova excavates the life of Frances “Fanny” Wright (1795-1852), the Scottish-born American reformer who fused scientific empiricism with radical abolitionism and free-love feminism. Popova argues that Wright’s forgotten 1829 Nashoba commune in Tennessee—an interracial, egalitarian settlement—prefigures contemporary debates on reparations, communal ownership, and the ethics of utopian experimentation. Whitman’s later recollection that she “possessed herself of my body and soul” is used to trace her influence on American poetic democracy.

Seamus Perry’s 2026 Winter Lecture, delivered at Conway Hall and now published in full, asks whether pluralism and poetic unity are irreconcilable. Taking Auden’s worry that a poem embodying liberal-democratic pluralism would be “formless, windy, banal and utterly boring” as his provocation, Perry reads late-modern poets from Jorie Graham to Geoffrey Hill to show how the “virtue of variousness” can coexist with formal rigour. The lecture positions the poem itself as a miniature polis whose internal negotiations mirror those of a diverse democracy.

Ben Rhodes reviews Philip and William Taubman’s “McNamara at War: A New History,” arguing that Robert McNamara’s lifelong penance for Vietnam never confronted the deeper sin of American exceptionalism. Rhodes contends that McNamara’s famous mea culpa—“We were wrong, terribly wrong”—still assumed the right of the United States to intervene anywhere. The review draws a direct line from McNamara’s technocratic hubris to Donald Trump’s current threats against Venezuela, Greenland and Iran, concluding that until the U.S. abandons the fantasy of its own indispensability, new Vietnams are inevitable.

triadic_ontology.findings: Two fresh 2026 papers advance the triadic turn. Christopher Isabelle’s “Triadic Relational Ontology: From Primordial Separation to Relational Field” (PhilArchive, 2026-04-29) argues that dyadic logic collapses without a third, actively-maintained relational field; separation alone yields only static difference, while the triadic structure supplies the continuity required for meaning and emergence. Kenneth Hammat’s “Consciousness Between Fact and Value: A Triadic Neurophenomenology” (February 17, 2026) proposes a testable model in which consciousness unifies three co-fundamental domains—object (material), idea (mental), and relation (axiological)—predicting distinct neural signatures for each and explaining why current AI simulates fact and idea yet lacks axiological participation. Both papers extend the lineage begun in Damiano Migliorini’s 2025 monograph “Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics: Subsistences, Events and Gunk,” which reframes the Trinity as a rational ontolog [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: Three major 2026 papers mark the maturation of autonomous research systems. InternAgent-1.5 (arXiv:2602.08990) presents a unified tri-subsystem architecture—generation, verification, evolution—capable of end-to-end discovery across wet-lab and computational domains, scoring top marks on GAIA, HLE and FrontierScience benchmarks while autonomously designing ML algorithms and running full empirical studies. Mimosa (arXiv:2603.28986) attacks the brittleness of fixed research workflows: a meta-orchestrator dynamically synthesizes and rewrites multi-agent topologies using Model Context Protocol tool discovery, achieving 43.1 % success on ScienceAgentBench and keeping complete audit trails for reproducibility. MARS (arXiv:2602.02660) focuses on budget-aware AI-research automation, combining cost-constrained Monte-Carlo tree search with reflective memory to overcome expensive model-training evaluations; it reaches SOTA on MLE-Bench and shows 63 % of insights emerge from cross-branch transfer.  [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congressional UAP disclosure efforts intensified in 2026 as President Trump directed federal agencies to release government files on extraterrestrial life and UAPs, with Defense Secretary Hegseth confirming Pentagon compliance. The House Oversight Committee's Task Force, led by Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, continues investigating AARO's transparency failures, demanding video files of UAP sightings near military installations. Recent hearings featured whistleblower David Grusch testifying under oath about non-human biologics discovered during UAP crash recoveries. The All-Domain Anomaly Resolution Office's caseload now exceeds 2,000 cases, while Congress considers new whistleblower protections in the FY26 NDAA. Despite AARO's claims of no extraterrestrial evidence, bipartisan momentum grows for declassification as lawmakers cite national security threats from unexplained aerial incursions over sensitive military sites.

newyorker.title: The Talk of the Town
newyorker.dek: Notes from the New Yorker staff on the Pope's historic meeting with the Archbishop of Canterbury, the latest on the Trump assassination attempt, and the growing influence of AI wearables in 2026.
newyorker.byline: Compiled by the New Yorker staff
newyorker.text:
Pope Leo's first encounter with Archbishop Sarah Mullally at the Vatican last week marked a watershed moment for Anglican-Catholic relations, with sources close to the Holy See describing the meeting as "warm beyond protocol." The two leaders reportedly spent ninety minutes in private discussion, focusing on shared concerns about declining church attendance across Europe and the theological implications of AI consciousness—a topic both have addressed in recent sermons. The Vatican's official statement emphasized "mutual recognition of pastoral challenges in an age of algorithmic mediation," while Canterbury insiders noted Mullally's particular interest in how AI might transform liturgical music, a subject close to her heart as former Chief Nursing Officer.

Meanwhile, the Secret Service has revised its protective protocols following Saturday's assassination attempt at the White House Correspondents' Dinner. New details reveal the 31-year-old suspect, now identified as former Silicon Valley engineer Marcus Chen, had been researching AI-powered targeting systems in the weeks leading up to the incident. Investigators discovered Chen had modified commercially available smart glasses with facial recognition software, raising fresh questions about the regulation of AI-enhanced consumer devices. The incident has prompted bipartisan calls in Congress for tighter oversight of wearable AI technology, with Senator Maria Cantwell (D-WA) proposing legislation that would require background checks for purchases of AI-enabled devices with recording capabilities.

The AI wearable boom shows no signs of slowing, with New York fashion week featuring several AI pendants on the runway this month. Designer Zac Posen's collaboration with startup Friend.com produced a necklace that reportedly analyzes conversation patterns to suggest optimal networking strategies—a feature that proved eerily prescient when it correctly predicted which gala attendees would secure venture funding. Critics have raised privacy concerns, noting these devices essentially create perpetual surveillance networks among the elite. Yet adoption continues apace, with one prominent venture capitalist observed wearing three different AI pendants simultaneously at a recent SoHo dinner party, each allegedly serving distinct analytical functions: one for deal flow, one for social dynamics, and one for "emotional intelligence optimization."

The intersection of technology and spirituality has become an unexpected theme of 2026, with both Pope Leo and Archbishop Mullally privately consulting AI ethicists about the pastoral implications of neural enhancement. Sources suggest the Vatican is preparing a doctrinal statement on "algorithmic consciousness and the soul," while Canterbury is developing guidelines for AI-assisted pastoral care. This theological grappling with artificial intelligence represents perhaps the most significant religious engagement with technology since the Galileo affair, raising fundamental questions about the boundaries between human and machine, creator and creation.
